changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "To My Granddaughter"

TextENGNew: Forgive me for this unfilial departure.
changeReason: New captures '不孝' (unfilial) -- a culturally significant term about dying before one's descendants -- which the original omits entirely.

TextENGNew: With so little time left, my consciousness fading, I would rather set out on the journey to the next world on my own two feet than wait helplessly, not knowing when death will claim me. I hope you can understand that.
changeReason: New accurately renders the Japanese: fading consciousness (薄れる意識), setting out on the journey to death on one's own feet (自らの足で死出の旅路), and not knowing when death will come. The original oversimplifies to 'take my own life.'

TextENGNew: At least this way, I can leave these words behind.
changeReason: Original says 'writing this letter to you'; the Japanese says 'leave these words behind' (文を残せる) -- the new translation correctly emphasizes leaving a record, not addressing someone.

TextENGNew: I cannot bear the terror of dying with a mind dulled by pointless life support, unable to leave behind a single word.
changeReason: New more accurately captures 'pointless life support' (無意味な延命) and 'mind dulled' (寝惚けた意識). Also 'a single word' vs 'a will' better matches 何も残すことができない.

TextENGNew: And yet, in the end, I have left nothing behind.
changeReason: Original adds 'for you' and uses future tense ('can leave'); the Japanese uses past tense ('could not leave' / 残せなかった) and doesn't specify a recipient.

TextENGNew: My life's work, my accomplishments, the crystallization of my efforts--none of it remains in this world.
changeReason: New preserves all three items from the Japanese (人生も誇るべき実績も結晶も -- life's work, achievements, crystallization of efforts); the original collapses these into just 'achievements.'

TextENGNew: I was not forgotten after my death. I was forgotten while I still lived.
changeReason: New preserves the two-part rhetorical structure of the Japanese: 'not forgotten after death, but forgotten while still alive,' which the original compresses into a single statement.

TextENGNew: The agony of departing this world while witnessing that erasure is beyond what words can express.
changeReason: Original adds 'humiliated man' and 'no appreciation from anyone' not in the Japanese. The JPN says the pain of departing while witnessing that erasure is beyond words (筆舌に尽くし難い).
Original says 'do better than me'; the Japanese specifically says 祖父を越えなさい ('surpass your grandfather'), using the third-person relationship term.

TextENGNew: You must reach the heights I could not attain.

changeReason: Original says 'leave your mark on this world' (figurative); the Japanese 後世に名前を残しなさい specifically means 'leave your name to posterity.'

TextENGNew: If that proves impossible, then leave behind great deeds and lasting achievements.
changeReason: Original drops the conditional clause 'if that proves impossible' (それが無理なら) present in the Japanese, changing the logical structure.

TextENGNew: As beings of flesh, both you and I shall eventually die.
changeReason: Original says only 'You will eventually die someday,' dropping 'As beings of flesh' (人の身である以上) and 'both you and I' (私もお前も) from the Japanese.

TextENGNew: As beings of flesh, we shall be cremated and reduced to ash.@k It is the destiny of mortal bodies to become ash.
changeReason: New accurately captures 'cremated' (焼かれて) and 'mortal bodies' (肉の身) from the Japanese, and uses 'we' rather than 'you' to match the inclusive phrasing.

TextENGNew: But if you accomplish great deeds and leave your name behind, you will live forever even after you become ash.
changeReason: New adds 'leave your name behind' (名を残したなら) and 'become ash' (灰となっても) from the Japanese, both missing in the original.

TextENGNew: When one continues to live after losing their mortal form, people call that being a god.
Your grandfather wished to become one, but could not reach that summit.
changeReason: Original says 'I wished'; the Japanese says 祖父 ('your grandfather'), maintaining third-person self-reference consistent with the letter's voice.
Original adds 'So in my place' and 'someday' not present in the Japanese, which simply says お前は神になりなさい ('You must become a god').
